title: Acids and bases — the chemistry of pH and neutralisation
examTip: AQA Paper 1, Topic 4 (Chemical Changes). Expect questions on the pH scale, writing word and symbol equations for neutralisation, naming salts, and predicting products. Required practical: making a salt by neutralisation.
introTips: Covers the pH scale, neutralisation reactions, and making salts
You'll be able to write equations for all four routes to making salts
By the end you can name any salt given the acid and base used
intro: Acids and bases are behind some of the most important chemistry in everyday life — from the acid in your stomach to the limestone used to neutralise acidic lakes. The reactions in this topic come up in almost every Paper 1, and knowing how to name salts and write equations correctly is one of the most reliable ways to pick up marks. The required practical on making a salt is also a favourite for 6-mark questions.

keyPoints.heading: The pH scale — acids, alkalis and strong vs weak
keyPoints.content: The pH scale runs from 0 to 14 and measures the concentration of hydrogen ions (H⁺) in solution. A pH below 7 is acidic — the lower the number, the more acidic (more H⁺ ions). pH 7 is neutral (pure water). A pH above 7 is alkaline — the higher the number, the more alkaline (more OH⁻ ions). Strong acids (e.g. hydrochloric acid, HCl; sulfuric acid, H₂SO₄; nitric acid, HNO₃) completely ionise in water, releasing all their H⁺ ions. Weak acids (e.g. ethanoic acid in vinegar, citric acid) only partially ionise — they reach an equilibrium where most of the molecules remain intact. A strong acid and a weak acid at the same concentration will have different pH values — the strong acid will have the lower (more acidic) pH.
writeBullets: pH 0–6 — acidic; the lower the pH, the higher the H⁺ ion concentration
pH 7 — neutral (pure water)
pH 8–14 — alkaline; the higher the pH, the higher the OH⁻ ion concentration
Strong acid — fully ionises in water (e.g. HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃)
Weak acid — partially ionises; equilibrium mixture (e.g. ethanoic acid, citric acid)
Same concentration — strong acid has lower pH than weak acid

keyTerms.def: A scale from 0–14 measuring acidity/alkalinity based on H⁺ ion concentration
An acid that completely ionises in water, releasing all its H⁺ ions
An acid that only partially ionises in water — an equilibrium exists between the ionised and un-ionised forms
A soluble base that produces OH⁻ ions in solution

keyPoints.memTip: 0 is most acidic (battery acid!), 14 is most alkaline (bleach). pH 7 = neutral = water. Cover and write the definition of a strong acid and a weak acid from memory, including an example of each.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Students often confuse 'strong' with 'concentrated'. A strong acid fully ionises regardless of how dilute it is. A weak acid is still 'weak' even if it's concentrated. AQA specifically tests this distinction.

keyPoints.heading: Neutralisation — acid + base → salt + water
keyPoints.content: A neutralisation reaction occurs when an acid reacts with a base to form a salt and water. The general equation is: acid + base → salt + water. A base is any substance that neutralises an acid. An alkali is a soluble base. At the ionic level, neutralisation is always: H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l). This is the ionic equation for all neutralisation reactions. Indicators (e.g. litmus, universal indicator) show when the solution has been neutralised — the endpoint is when the indicator shows pH 7. In a titration, you add acid to alkali (or vice versa) from a burette to find the exact volume needed for neutralisation.
writeBullets: Neutralisation — acid + base → salt + water
Base — any substance that neutralises an acid (includes metal oxides, hydroxides, carbonates)
Alkali — a soluble base that produces OH⁻ ions in solution
Ionic equation — H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l) (all neutralisation reactions)
Indicator — shows endpoint; universal indicator turns green at pH 7
Titration — precise method to find exact volume of acid/alkali needed for neutralisation

keyTerms.def: The reaction between an acid and a base to produce a salt and water
A substance that neutralises an acid; includes metal oxides, hydroxides and carbonates
A soluble base that produces OH⁻ ions in solution (e.g. sodium hydroxide)
A technique to find the exact volume of acid or alkali needed to neutralise a known volume of the other

keyPoints.memTip: The ionic equation H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O never changes — it's the same for every acid-alkali neutralisation. Write it five times, then cover and write it from memory with state symbols.
keyPoints.tutor_note: In 6-mark questions on the required practical, describe: measuring fixed volume of alkali with a pipette, adding indicator, adding acid from burette drop by drop near endpoint, swirling, recording the volume at the colour change. This is the method AQA wants.

keyPoints.heading: Four routes to making salts
keyPoints.content: There are four main reactions that produce salts: (1) Acid + metal: acid + metal → salt + hydrogen. Example: HCl + Mg → MgCl₂ + H₂. Only works with metals above hydrogen in the reactivity series. (2) Acid + metal oxide: acid + metal oxide → salt + water. Example: H₂SO₄ + CuO → CuSO₄ + H₂O. (3) Acid + metal carbonate: acid + metal carbonate → salt + water + carbon dioxide. Example: HCl + CaCO₃ → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂. The CO₂ can be tested with limewater — it turns milky. (4) Acid + alkali (neutralisation): HCl + NaOH → NaCl + H₂O. This is used in titrations because there is no visible reaction to show completion — an indicator or pH meter is needed.
writeBullets: Acid + metal → salt + hydrogen (only if metal is above H in reactivity series)
Acid + metal oxide → salt + water
Acid + metal carbonate → salt + water + carbon dioxide
Acid + alkali → salt + water (no visible sign — use indicator or pH meter)
CO₂ test — bubbles through limewater; milky/cloudy result confirms carbon dioxide
H₂ test — lighted splint gives squeaky pop

keyTerms.def: An ionic compound formed when the H⁺ of an acid is replaced by a metal ion
Bubble the gas through limewater; it turns cloudy/milky if CO₂ is present
A compound containing a metal and the carbonate ion (CO₃²⁻); reacts with acid to give salt, water and CO₂

keyPoints.memTip: Remember the 4 routes as: Metal, Metal Oxide, Metal Carbonate, Alkali — each gives a salt. Carbonate is the only one that gives CO₂. Cover and write the word equation for acid + metal carbonate from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: When a question asks you to choose a method for making a specific salt, think about which is most practical: if the salt is soluble and there's no obvious precipitate, use titration (acid + alkali). If one reactant is insoluble (like a carbonate), filter off the excess.

keyPoints.heading: Naming salts — acid determines the anion
keyPoints.content: The name of a salt has two parts: the metal (or ammonium) part comes first, then the acid-derived part. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) → salts are chlorides. Sulfuric acid (H₂SO₄) → salts are sulfates. Nitric acid (HNO₃) → salts are nitrates. For example: HCl + NaOH → sodium chloride + H₂O; H₂SO₄ + KOH → potassium sulfate + H₂O; HNO₃ + Ca(OH)₂ → calcium nitrate + H₂O. The metal in the salt always comes from the base or metal used. The name of the acid always determines the anion (the negative ion).
writeBullets: Hydrochloric acid → chloride salts (e.g. NaCl = sodium chloride)
Sulfuric acid → sulfate salts (e.g. MgSO₄ = magnesium sulfate)
Nitric acid → nitrate salts (e.g. Ca(NO₃)₂ = calcium nitrate)
Salt name = metal name + acid-derived anion
Metal comes from the base/metal used in the reaction
Ammonium salts — if ammonia (NH₃) is used as the base: e.g. ammonium chloride

keyTerms.def: The anion (Cl⁻) formed in salts made from hydrochloric acid
The anion (SO₄²⁻) formed in salts made from sulfuric acid
The anion (NO₃⁻) formed in salts made from nitric acid

keyPoints.memTip: HCl = cHloride (both have the H). H₂SO₄ = sulfate. HNO₃ = nitrate. Cover and name the salt formed when: (a) HCl + MgO, (b) H₂SO₄ + KOH, (c) HNO₃ + CaCO₃.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Never write 'hydrochloride' — the salt is a 'chloride'. A common error is writing the acid name rather than changing it properly. Just learn: -chloric/HCl → chloride; sulfuric → sulfate; nitric → nitrate.

keyPoints.heading: Soluble and insoluble salts — precipitation reactions
keyPoints.content: Not all salts are soluble in water. When two soluble solutions are mixed and form an insoluble product, this is a precipitation reaction — the insoluble solid that forms is called a precipitate. For example: lead nitrate solution + potassium iodide solution → lead iodide precipitate (yellow solid) + potassium nitrate solution. Solubility rules to know: most sodium, potassium, and ammonium salts are soluble; most nitrates are soluble; most chlorides are soluble except lead chloride and silver chloride; most sulfates are soluble except barium sulfate, lead sulfate, and calcium sulfate; most carbonates and hydroxides are insoluble except those of sodium, potassium and ammonium. Precipitation is used to remove unwanted ions from solution, e.g. in water treatment.
writeBullets: Precipitation — two soluble solutions mix; an insoluble salt (precipitate) forms
Precipitate — the insoluble solid that falls out of solution
Most nitrates — soluble; most carbonates — insoluble
Most chlorides — soluble; except AgCl and PbCl₂
Most sulfates — soluble; except BaSO₄, PbSO₄, CaSO₄
Use — removing unwanted ions from solution (e.g. water treatment)

keyTerms.def: A reaction where two solutions are mixed and an insoluble solid (precipitate) forms
An insoluble solid that forms when two solutions are mixed
Rules that predict whether a salt will dissolve in water (e.g. all nitrates are soluble)

keyPoints.memTip: PANSS — most nitrates (N), sodium (S), potassium (K), ammonium (A) salts are soluble. Exceptions for chlorides: AgCl and PbCl₂. Exceptions for sulfates: BaSO₄, PbSO₄, CaSO₄. Cover and write the exceptions from memory.
keyPoints.tutor_note: Precipitation questions often ask for an ionic equation. Identify the precipitate, write the ions that form it, then cancel the spectator ions. Example: Pb²⁺(aq) + 2I⁻(aq) → PbI₂(s) for lead iodide precipitation.

tables.title: Common acid + base combinations and salts formed

0.question: A solution has a pH of 3. Which statement correctly describes it?
options: It is alkaline with a high concentration of OH⁻ ions
It is neutral — neither acidic nor alkaline
It is acidic with a high concentration of H⁺ ions
It is a strong alkali because 3 is close to neutral
0.correctFeedback: Correct — pH 3 is acidic. The lower the pH below 7, the higher the concentration of H⁺ ions.
0.wrongFeedback: A pH of 3 is well below 7, which means the solution is acidic. Acidic solutions have a high concentration of H⁺ ions. Alkaline solutions have a high concentration of OH⁻ ions and a pH above 7.

2.question: Which products are formed when hydrochloric acid reacts with calcium carbonate?
options: Calcium oxide, carbon dioxide and water
Calcium chloride, water and carbon dioxide
2.correctFeedback: Correct — acid + metal carbonate gives salt + water + carbon dioxide. HCl + CaCO₃ → CaCl₂ + H₂O + CO₂.
2.wrongFeedback: Acid + metal carbonate always gives three products: a salt, water, and carbon dioxide. So hydrochloric acid + calcium carbonate → calcium chloride + water + carbon dioxide. The CO₂ is the key difference from acid + metal oxide (which gives only salt + water).

3.question: What is the name of the salt formed when sulfuric acid reacts with potassium hydroxide?
3.correctFeedback: Correct — sulfuric acid always produces sulfate salts. The metal comes from the base (potassium from potassium hydroxide), so the salt is potassium sulfate.
3.wrongFeedback: The acid determines the anion: sulfuric acid produces sulfates. The metal comes from the base — here potassium from potassium hydroxide. So the salt is potassium sulfate (K₂SO₄). Remember: sulfuric → sulfate, hydrochloric → chloride, nitric → nitrate.

commonMistakes: Confusing 'strong' with 'concentrated' — a strong acid fully ionises regardless of concentration; a concentrated acid just has a high amount dissolved per litre.
Forgetting that acid + carbonate produces CO₂ as well as salt and water — three products, not two.
Writing 'hydrochloride' instead of 'chloride' as the salt anion — the correct name is always just 'chloride', 'sulfate', or 'nitrate'.
Missing CO₂ from the word equation for acid + carbonate reactions.
In ionic equations for neutralisation, writing H⁺ + OH⁻ → H₂O without state symbols — AQA may require H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l).
examTips: In salt naming questions, identify the acid first (hydrochloric → chloride; sulfuric → sulfate; nitric → nitrate) then add the metal name from the base.
For the required practical method, describe using a pipette for the fixed volume, a burette for the added solution, and swirling near the endpoint — these are the details AQA marks.
When writing the equation for acid + carbonate, always include CO₂ as a product and check that CO₂ is balanced correctly.
The ionic equation for all acid-alkali neutralisations is H⁺(aq) + OH⁻(aq) → H₂O(l) — learn it and use it directly.
Precipitation ionic equations: identify the insoluble product, write the two ions that form it, and cancel spectator ions — they stay (aq) on both sides.
revisionCardBullets: pH 0–6 = acidic (high H⁺); pH 7 = neutral; pH 8–14 = alkaline (high OH⁻)
Strong acid — fully ionises in water (HCl, H₂SO₄, HNO₃)
Weak acid — partially ionises; equilibrium (e.g. ethanoic acid)
Acid + metal carbonate → salt + water + CO₂
HCl → chloride salts; H₂SO₄ → sulfate salts; HNO₃ → nitrate salts
Precipitation — two solutions form an insoluble salt (precipitate)
Most nitrates are soluble; most carbonates are insoluble
AgCl and BaSO₄ are insoluble — used in precipitation reactions
